claim: YOLO demand evidence dispatch implements boundary audit through snapshot-based change detection that blocks PRD readiness on target-project mutation, but lacks runtime mutation prevention during agent execution.

evidence.summary: buildBoundarySnapshot creates SHA256 hashes of all project files except allowed write roots; diffBoundarySnapshots detects added/modified/deleted files by comparing before/after snapshots.
evidence.why: Core boundary audit mechanism that detects project mutations after agent execution completes.

evidence.summary: Runtime captures boundary snapshot before agent execution (line 443), runs agent (lines 446-485), captures after snapshot (line 486), diffs to detect changes (line 487), and populates result.boundary with mutation status and changes (lines 488-492).
evidence.why: Demonstrates complete boundary audit workflow from pre-execution snapshot through change detection to result reporting.

evidence.summary: Boundary violations generate BOUNDARY_PROJECT_MUTATION blockers added to readiness.blockers array, forcing prd_ready=false and blocking PRD progression.
evidence.why: Proves that boundary audit results directly control PRD readiness state per the acceptance criteria.

evidence.summary: Execution policy specifies project_mutation: 'forbidden', allowed_write_roots restricted to evidence output directory only, writes_business_code: false, agent_instruction: 'boundary_controlled'.
evidence.why: Shows the policy contract that boundary audit enforces, though these are declarative constraints without runtime enforcement.

evidence.summary: Agent prompt explicitly states 'Boundary: do not modify target project files. If verification would require mutation, report it in missing' and specifies allowed artifact root.
evidence.why: Demonstrates that boundary restrictions are communicated to agents via prompt instructions rather than runtime technical constraints.

evidence.summary: executionConfig normalizes agentToolProfile to 'research', 'full', or 'boundary' based on agentToolProfile value and allowFullAgentTools flag; applies DEMAND_EVIDENCE_CLAUDE_BOUNDARY_SETTINGS for Claude provider.
evidence.why: Shows tool profile configuration logic that determines which tool restrictions apply to evidence agents.

evidence.summary: DEMAND_EVIDENCE_CLAUDE_BOUNDARY_SETTINGS defines CLAUDE_BOUNDARY_TOOLS including Read, Write, Edit, Bash, etc., showing agents have access to modification tools.
evidence.why: Critical evidence that agents technically CAN modify project files, making boundary audit essential since prevention is not technical.

evidence.summary: Claude provider invocation uses --allowedTools and --disallowedTools flags for tool control, but evidence-dispatch passes CLAUDE_BOUNDARY_TOOLS as allowed.
evidence.why: Confirms that tool restrictions are implemented via Claude Code flags, not filesystem-level barriers.

evidence.summary: DEMAND_EVIDENCE_AGENT_PROTOCOLS defines explorer, cross_checker, and verifier protocols with writes_code: false, specifying evidence-only mandates.
evidence.why: Shows the protocol contract that evidence agents must not modify files, though this is not technically enforced.

assumptions: Agent prompt instructions ('do not modify target project files') are sufficient to guide agent behavior in most cases
SHA256 file hashing provides reliable change detection for binary and text files
Git file listing (git ls-files) or filesystem walk covers all relevant project files
Claude Code tool permission flags (--allowedTools) effectively restrict available tools
Post-execution boundary diff happens quickly enough to detect changes before they could be committed or cause damage
risks: Boundary audit is post-execution only; mutations occur before detection, allowing potential damage during agent execution window
Agent prompt instructions are not technically enforced—agents with full tool access can ignore boundaries
No runtime sandbox or filesystem isolation prevents writes to project files during agent execution
If allowFullAgentTools=true with agentToolProfile='full', agents have unrestricted Write/Edit/Bash access despite boundary policy
Dirty working tree (existing modified files) could complicate change detection if not properly excluded from snapshots
Boundary blockers appear only after execution completes; user cannot prevent mutations, only react to them
missing: Verification that boundary audit has been stress-tested with real Claude -p agents in dirty working tree state
Evidence of runtime testing where agents intentionally violated boundaries to verify detection
Documentation or tests showing boundary snapshot accuracy across file types (binary, symlinks, special files)
Verification that CLAUDE_BOUNDARY_TOOLS list is current and complete for Claude Code capabilities
Evidence that execution mode detection (research vs full vs boundary) is tested and validated

result.notes: Boundary audit implementation is complete and correct for post-execution change detection. The core claim is verified: evidence dispatch uses SHA256 snapshots to detect project mutations and blocks PRD readiness when changes occur. However, the system lacks runtime mutation prevention—boundaries are enforced via detection after execution, not prevention during execution. This is a design limitation, not an implementation bug. The missing evidence items suggest the system needs stress-testing validation rather than code changes.
